identified_error: The study used change scores (postoperative minus preoperative) as dependent variables in multiple linear regression without controlling for baseline (preoperative) scores, violating the principle of regression to the mean and introducing bias. Specifically, patients with lower preoperative scores have more room to improve, creating an artificial negative correlation between baseline scores and improvement.
why_fatal: This error invalidates the conclusions because the identified 'predictors' (deep posterior compartment involvement, younger age, depression, male sex) are likely confounded by baseline differences. For example, patients with depression or those with deep posterior compartment involvement might have had more severe preoperative symptoms (lower baseline FAAM/VAS scores), giving them greater potential for improvement. The regression analysis attributes this mathematical artifact to patient characteristics, leading to false conclusions about which factors predict better outcomes.
correct_approach: The correct approach would be to use postoperative scores as the dependent variable while including preoperative scores as a covariate in the regression model (ANCOVA approach). Alternatively, a repeated-measures analysis could be used. This controls for baseline differences and assesses whether patient characteristics predict postoperative outcome after accounting for preoperative status.
additional_errors: Retrospective collection of preoperative PROs introduces recall bias.
58% response rate for postoperative surveys risks non-response bias (those with poor outcomes may be less likely to respond).
Very small subgroup with depression (n=5) makes regression coefficient for depression unreliable.
Wide range of follow-up (12-115 months) introduces variability not accounted for in analysis.
Multiple comparisons across three outcome measures without adjustment increases Type I error risk.
Case series design without control group limits causal inference about treatment effectiveness.
